competencies.justification: The chapter's fundamental organising purpose is to trace how sociocultural beliefs and concepts (including ahimsa, karma, rebirth, brahman/atman, anekantavada) changed over time from early Vedic culture through Buddhism and Jainism. Every major named section (a. What are the Vedas?, b. Vedic society, c. Vedic schools of thought, Buddhism, Jainism) is architecturally dedicated to explaining these changes and their origin. C-3.1 explicitly names 'ahimsa' and 'sociocultural beliefs and concepts over time' as exemplar demands; the chapter's architecture compels students to execute exactly this analysis across its full span. Removing this competency would dissolve the chapter's structural spine.

Explaining major historical changes and their societal impact is developed substantively across multiple named sections: the evolution of Vedic culture into Upaniṣhadic thought (section c), the emergence of Buddhism and Jainism as departures from Vedic authority (dedicated sections), and the enduring impact of these movements on Indian and Asian civilisation. The chapter's own chronological organising logic (with a timeline from 1500 BCE to 500 CE) makes continuity of this competency evident across sections.

The chapter continuously demonstrates that ancient beliefs, practices, and values have persisted despite major societal changes: Vedic chanting recognised by UNESCO in 2008 after 100-200 generations of oral transmission; Buddhism still major in parts of India and Asia; tribal deities (e.g., Jagannath) absorbed into mainstream Hinduism while tribal communities continue ancient worship practices (Donyipolo, Singbonga). This thread of continued prevalence runs through every named section of the chapter.

The chapter's opening metaphor (Indian culture as a tree with many roots and branches, united by a common trunk) and closing synthesis ('Before we move on') are architecturally organised to explain India's cultural unity-in-diversity. Across all sections the chapter shows how diverse schools — Vedic, Buddhist, Jain, tribal — share a common trunk of concepts (dharma, karma, rebirth, ahimsa, the search for truth and end of ignorance), directly illustrating C-7.1's demand to recognise commonalities in philosophical ideas and values. C-7.1 explicitly names 'yoga' which is introduced in section c.

Section 'a. What are the Vedas?' is a named subsection whose primary subject is the Vedas as ancient cultural-historical sources to be comprehended and interpreted — their etymology, composition, oral transmission over millennia, UNESCO recognition, and the worldview they encode. This is a named structural element whose primary subject requires students to engage with and interpret primary cultural source material, present but not developed as a dedicated section.

The 'Folk and Tribal Roots' section (including the 'What is a tribe?' subsection) is a named structural element whose primary subject is India's tradition of inclusion and mutual enrichment across communities — showing how tribal, folk, and Hindu traditions have freely exchanged deities, concepts, and rituals across millennia. Andre Beteille's quotation explicitly frames this as a mutual, bidirectional influence. The Buddhism section also notes the religion's spread across India and Asia. These are named structural elements requiring students to appreciate India's tradition of inclusion, present but not developed as a dedicated section.

Section 'a. What are the Vedas?' includes the DON'T MISS OUT sidebar explicitly presenting UNESCO's 2008 recognition of Vedic chanting as India's contribution to 'the oral and intangible heritage of humanity'. The Buddhism section shows Indian philosophical contributions (ahimsa, enlightenment) spreading across Asia. The Jainism section presents anekantavada as an Indian intellectual contribution. These are named structural elements (subsection + sidebar) whose primary subject includes India's contributions to humanity, present but not developed as a dedicated section.
